name: Framer
tagline: Design and publish websites without code
description: Framer is a design and website publishing tool that blurs the line between design and production. You can design with component-level control and publish directly to the web — no developers needed. Popular for portfolio sites, landing pages, and startup websites.
cons: Page view limits on lower plans
use_cases: You're a designer who wants to ship polished, production-ready websites without handing off to a developer
Your startup needs a high-converting landing page with scroll animations and micro-interactions that Webflow or Squarespace can't easily produce
You want pixel-level design control with Figma-like component logic but published directly to the web
You're building a portfolio or personal site where visual quality matters more than CMS depth

plan_guidance.best_for: Designers evaluating Framer before publishing, or building and testing prototypes locally. The 100 page view per month limit makes the Free plan unusable for any live site — it's a trial tier, not a viable publishing option.
plan_guidance.upgrade_when: You're ready to share your site publicly. Even a portfolio linked from a social media bio can exhaust 100 views within hours of being shared.

plan_guidance.best_for: Personal portfolios or very small sites with organic-only traffic. Suitable if you post your URL occasionally and don't run any paid traffic or social campaigns to it. Custom domain access at $5/mo makes this the entry point for real publishing.
plan_guidance.upgrade_when: Your site receives more than 1,000 monthly visitors, you need a second editor on the project, or you need to password-protect pages — all of which require Basic.

plan_guidance.best_for: Freelancers, consultants, and agencies building marketing sites for clients. Password protection for client previews, 3 collaborators, and 10,000 monthly page views covers most professional use cases. This is the right tier for the majority of commercial Framer sites.
plan_guidance.upgrade_when: You need a blog, case study archive, or any dynamically managed content — CMS collections are only available on Pro. Basic is static-only.

plan_guidance.best_for: Startups, product teams, and agencies that need CMS-powered content, staging environments for client review, and higher traffic capacity. The right tier for any content-driven site where the team publishes regularly.
plan_guidance.upgrade_when: You're consistently exceeding 200,000 monthly page views. At that scale, contact Framer directly for enterprise pricing — the Pro plan's hard cap can cause performance issues during viral traffic spikes.

hidden_costs: CMS collections — needed for blogs, case studies, or any content managed outside the editor — are only available on Pro ($30/mo). Basic and Mini plans are static-only.
Custom code components using React require Basic or above. Free and Mini plans cannot embed React components or custom scripts.
Framer has no native form backend. You'll need a third-party tool (Tally, Typeform, or a paid plan of any form builder) to handle contact forms, survey submissions, or lead capture.

Page view hard caps can catch you off-guard: a Product Hunt launch or viral social post can exhaust a month's Pro allocation (200K views) in under 24 hours, triggering throttling before you can upgrade.

extra_faqs.q: Does Framer have a CMS for blog posts or dynamic content?
extra_faqs.a: Yes — but only on the Pro plan ($30/mo). Basic and Mini plans support static pages built in the editor only. If you need to publish blog articles, case studies, team member pages, or any content managed from a CMS interface, you must be on Pro. This is the most common reason people upgrade from Basic: they outgrow static pages and need a manageable content layer.

extra_faqs.q: What happens when I hit Framer's page view limit?
extra_faqs.a: Framer doesn't immediately take your site offline, but will throttle performance and display an upgrade prompt in your dashboard. The Free plan's 100 views/mo limit is easily exceeded — a single shared link on Twitter or LinkedIn can exhaust it in minutes. The Mini plan's 1,000 views/mo is practical for a quiet portfolio but fails quickly if you run any paid traffic, social campaigns, or appear in search results consistently.
